title: Project / Scope Picker UX — Rally, Jira, ADO, Linear
topic: Rally Software project picker UX — chrome placement, keyboard navigation, recent/pinned nodes, search-as-you-type, descendants-included scoping, URL persistence, breadcrumb behaviour. Also cross-compare with Jira project switcher, Azure DevOps area-path picker, and Linear workspace switcher.
summary: How Rally, Jira, Azure DevOps, and Linear surface project/scope pickers — chrome placement, hierarchy, descendants, URL.
content:
Contents
  • Overview
  • Key Findings
  • Rally Software
  • Jira
  • Azure DevOps
  • Linear
  • Comparison Table
  • Patterns Worth Copying
  • Anti-patterns Observed
  • Gaps
  • Sources

Overview

Vector is building topology-scoped navigation where users switch between hierarchical org nodes (offices, portfolios, teams) — a problem structurally identical to the project/scope picker in leading PPM and dev-tools. This paper surveys how Rally, Jira, Azure DevOps, and Linear each solve chrome placement, hierarchy display, descendants scoping, keyboard navigation, and URL persistence, so that Vector's picker design is grounded in prior art rather than invented from scratch. The comparison is especially relevant to the useArtefactItemsWindow scope-generic surface introduced in B21 (PLA-0037).

Key Findings

  • Rally is the only tool with bidirectional rollup — users can scope to parent projects, child projects, or both directions simultaneously via explicit checkboxes; no other tool offers upward rollup.
  • ADO has the deepest tree (up to 14 levels) but splits scoping across two separate pickers — the chrome project switcher and the per-team Area Path admin UI — creating a navigational seam that the investigation flags as an anti-pattern.
  • Linear leads on keyboard navigation — Cmd-K opens a fuzzy command menu reachable from anywhere, and O then W switches workspaces; no other tool documents a dedicated picker keyboard shortcut.
  • Jira is the only flat model — projects are not nested, so descendants are not a concept; it compensates with Starred (drag-reorderable) and Recent lists in the sidebar.
  • URL persistence of scope is universal except Rally — Jira, ADO, and Linear all encode the project/team key in the URL path; Rally's URL behaviour is not documented in official sources.
  • Permission inheritance varies sharply — ADO supports per-node ACLs that inherit downward (with a documented overhead warning), Linear auto-inherits parent-team access for sub-team members, Jira uses per-project schemes with no inheritance, and Rally filters by workspace visibility.
  • Search-as-you-type is present only in Jira and Linear (via their command palettes); Rally and ADO do not document search within the chrome picker itself.
  • Breadcrumb coupling differs — Rally shows an explicit scope indicator below the picker; ADO treats the breadcrumb as the picker itself; Jira and Linear surface scope implicitly through sidebar selection state.

Rally Software

Placement in the chrome

The picker lives in the upper-left corner of every page as a persistent control on the top navigation bar. "Select a project from the drop-down list located in the upper-left corner" (Source 1). It is a persistent dropdown control, not hidden behind a menu.

Trigger

The user clicks the project name in the top-left to open the dropdown.

Search-as-you-type

Not documented in the official sources surfaced by this investigation. A search of techdocs.broadcom.com did not surface a typed-search description for the picker itself.

Recent / pinned / favourites

Both "Recently Used" and personalised pinning are supported. "Customize your Rally experience by saving the pages most relevant to you in your navigation sidebar… If you work with or support multiple teams, you can save those teams in your My Projects selector for quick access to any relevant pages for them" (Source 7). A "My Projects" selector is the user-pinned bucket.

Hierarchy display

An indented tree of nodes, with the workspace as the top container and projects as nested children. "The hierarchy that you are trying to represent must mirror your organizational structure" (Source 4) and "Always add a node at the top of your hierarchy that represents your company or division" (Source 4). The picker container includes the project tree plus optional scope checkboxes below it (Source 2, Source 5).

Descendants behaviour

Explicit toggles — descendants are not included by default; the user opts in with checkboxes. "In the Show Items From section, select Parent Projects, Child Projects or both" (Source 2) and "You can use the Show items from check boxes in the Project drop-down list to choose additional work items" (Source 2). Rally is unique among the four tools in surfacing bidirectional rollup: "You can specify to view work items from projects above, below, or both directions in the project hierarchy" (Source 2). Parent projects "contain… work items that are shared across all associated child projects. The parent project includes all levels of child projects" (Source 2).

URL persistence

Not documented in the official sources surfaced by this investigation. Rally's SaaS URLs historically include a project OID query param, but no canonical doc page describes URL behaviour on switch.

Permission inheritance

Implicit — projects inherit through the workspace > project tree, but the docs do not give a clean one-sentence rule. Closed workspaces no longer display in the drop-down list on the navigation bar or the project picker (Source 3), i.e. visibility-driven filtering.

Breadcrumb coupling

Yes — a scope indicator is shown below the picker. "An indicator of your selection shows below the project picker at the top of the page" (Source 2). When rollups are active the indicator names how many parents/children are scoped in.

Empty state / no scope

Not documented in the official sources surfaced by this investigation.

Jira

Placement in the chrome

Left sidebar (Spaces). "Navigation starts from the sidebar" and "Work across multiple spaces and boards straight from the sidebar" (Source 8).

Trigger

Click Projects/Spaces in the sidebar, then a starred or recent project, or "View all projects". "If you have access to multiple spaces and/or boards, drop-down menus will become available in the sidebar, so you can quickly and easily switch between your spaces and boards" (Source 9). Atlassian also documents a command palette to "search work items, projects, and more with your keyboard" (Source 6).

Search-as-you-type

Yes — implicit via the global command palette (Source 6).

Recent / pinned / favourites

Both are supported. "Recent: Spaces, work items, boards, filters, and dashboards you've visited, edited, or created" and "Starred: Essential items you've marked with a star" (Source 8). Starred items can be drag-reordered: "you'll be able to reorder your starred projects in place… hover over a starred project in your sidebar, drag it to your desired position" (Source 11).

Hierarchy display

Flat list — Starred at top, Recent below. Projects in Jira are not nested.

Descendants behaviour

Not applicable — Jira projects are flat; there is no parent–child project tree.

URL persistence

Yes. The URL contains the project key, e.g. https://[SITE_NAME].atlassian.net/jira/software/c/projects/[PROJECT_KEY]/boards/[BOARD_ID] (Source 12).

Permission inheritance

Per-project permission schemes; no parent-tree inheritance because there is no parent.

Breadcrumb coupling

Not documented in the official sources surfaced by this investigation as a separate breadcrumb component below the picker.

Empty state / no scope

Not documented in the official sources surfaced by this investigation.

Azure DevOps

Key observation: ADO splits scoping into two separate pickers — the project switcher (in the top-left chrome) and the Area Path picker (per-team, lives in the breadcrumb at the top of Boards/Backlogs pages). This two-picker model is flagged as an anti-pattern in the cross-comparison.

Placement in the chrome

Project switcher: top-left breadcrumb chrome. Area Path picker: (a) Project settings > Project configuration > Areas admin tree, and (b) team selector in breadcrumb on Boards. "If you need to switch the team context, use the team selector within the breadcrumbs" (Source 13). "From your web portal, select (1) Project settings > (2) Project configuration > (3) Areas" (Source 13).

Trigger

Click the project name in the top breadcrumb, or click the team name in the breadcrumb.

Search-as-you-type

Not documented in the official sources surfaced by this investigation for the chrome picker.

Recent / pinned / favourites

Not documented in the official sources surfaced by this investigation for the project switcher.

Hierarchy display

Tree, up to 14 levels deep. "you can also create a hierarchical structure of area paths to support subareas, with up to 14 levels deep" (Source 13). "Path hierarchy depth — Must be fewer than 14 levels deep" (Source 14). Area path strings are backslash-separated, e.g. Fabrikam FiberAreaService DeliveryVoice (Source 13).

Descendants behaviour

Explicit per-team toggle — "Include sub areas" boolean per area-path row on each team. "You can select one or more area paths and optionally include their subarea paths. Choose to include subarea paths when you want to support rollup views of work done across several teams or areas" (Source 13). Up to 300 area paths per team.

URL persistence

Yes — https://dev.azure.com/{org}/{project}/_boards/.... Switching project replaces the {project} slug. The area path itself is usually not in the URL — the filter is applied per team (Source 15).

Permission inheritance

Permissions can be set per area-path node and inherit down. "To add, edit, and manage area paths under a node: One or more of the following permissions set to Allow: Create child nodes; Delete this node; Edit this node; View permissions in this node" (Source 13). Microsoft warns: "While you can use areas to partition permissions on work items, complex trees require significant overhead for permission management" (Source 14).

Breadcrumb coupling

Strong — team name and area path appear as breadcrumb segments at the top of every Boards page; switching scope is done via the same breadcrumb (Source 13).

Empty state / no scope

"New projects include a single root area corresponding to the project name and a default team with the same name assigned to this root area path" (Source 13).

Linear

Placement in the chrome

Workspace switcher: very top-left as dropdown next to workspace name. Teams (and projects): left sidebar. "you can switch between these accounts using the dropdown menu in the top left-hand corner of the application" (Source 17). "Teams you have joined will appear in your sidebar" (Source 18).

Trigger

Click the workspace name top-left. Keyboard: O then W to switch workspaces; Cmd/Ctrl-K opens the command menu (can fuzzy-search any team/project/view). Shortcut help opens with ? (Source 19).

Search-as-you-type

Yes — Cmd-K is canonical and fuzzy. "The command menu lets you search and take any actions applicable to your view or selection" (Source 16).

Recent / pinned / favourites

Yes — explicit Favorites section in sidebar. "Teams you navigate to that you are not a member of will show up in your sidebar under a temporary Exploring section" (Source 18).

Hierarchy display

Indented tree in sidebar. Linear added sub-teams in 2025. "Create parent teams and nest sub-teams under them to group teams by function, department, or product area" (Source 21). "The linear application sidebar showing 'Your teams' with a parent team named 'Mobile' and two child teams labeled 'iOS' and 'Android'… The child teams are nested and indented under the parent team" (Source 21).

Descendants behaviour

Automatic access inheritance. "Members of any sub-team automatically have access to their parent team, enabling cross-team visibility" (Source 21). "Sub-teams inherit workflows, cycles, and labels from their parent team" (Source 21). A workspace-level Projects page aggregates across teams: "There is also a Projects page at the workspace level so you can view all projects within your workspace in one location" (Source 22).

URL persistence

Yes. Pattern: https://linear.app/{workspace}/team/{TEAM_KEY}/... and issue URLs https://linear.app/{workspace}/issue/{TEAM_KEY}-{NUMBER}/.... The TEAM_KEY is the same short identifier used as the issue prefix.

Permission inheritance

Sub-teams auto-inherit parent access. "Members of any sub-team automatically have access to their parent team" (Source 21).

Breadcrumb coupling

Implicit — sidebar selection acts as the breadcrumb.

Empty state / no scope

Not documented in the official sources surfaced by this investigation.

Comparison Table

Dimension Rally Jira Azure DevOps Linear
Picker chrome location Top-left dropdown Left sidebar (Spaces) Top breadcrumb (project + team) Top-left dropdown + sidebar
Hierarchy model Workspace > project tree (deep) Flat (projects are not nested) Project > area path tree (up to 14 deep) Workspace > team > sub-team > project
Descendants by default No — explicit checkboxes N/A (no tree) Per-team Include sub areas boolean Yes — sub-team access inherits automatically
Bidirectional rollup Yes (unique) No Down-only via sub-area toggle Down-only
Search-as-you-type NOT FOUND Yes (command palette) NOT FOUND in chrome picker Yes (Cmd-K, fuzzy)
Pinned / favourites My Projects selector Starred (drag-reorderable) NOT FOUND Favorites sidebar group
Recent Recently used projects Recent section NOT FOUND Implicit via Cmd-K
Keyboard shortcut to open picker NOT FOUND NOT FOUND (dedicated) NOT FOUND O then W (workspace) + Cmd-K (any)
URL persists scope NOT FOUND in docs Yes — /projects/{KEY}/... Yes — dev.azure.com/{org}/{project}/_boards/... Yes — linear.app/{workspace}/team/{KEY}/...
Permission inheritance from tree Implicit (workspace visibility) Per-project schemes (no inheritance) Per-node ACL with downward inheritance Sub-team auto-inherits parent access
Breadcrumb beneath picker Yes — scope indicator below picker Implicit via sidebar Strong — breadcrumb IS the picker Implicit via sidebar

Patterns Worth Copying

  1. Rally's bidirectional scope toggle — allow users to scope to parents, children, or both directions simultaneously; uniquely powerful for rollup views in deep hierarchies.
  2. ADO's breadcrumb-as-picker — treating the breadcrumb as an active switching control eliminates the need for a separate chrome widget; scope is always visible and always actionable.
  3. Linear's Cmd-K everywhere + Favorites — a single fuzzy command menu accessible from any context, combined with an explicit Favorites group in the sidebar, covers both power-user and casual workflows.
  4. Jira's Starred + Recent + drag-reorder — drag-reorderable starred items give users a personalised fast lane without requiring a dedicated pinning UI.
  5. URL slug = team/project key (Jira, ADO, Linear) — encoding the scope in the URL path makes links shareable and bookmarkable by default; Rally is the outlier and loses this benefit.

Anti-patterns Observed

  • Two-picker model (ADO) — splitting project selection (chrome switcher) and area-path scoping (team settings admin UI) into two separate controls creates a navigational seam; users must visit a settings page to change what work they see on a board.
  • Hiding scope behind a settings page (ADO admin Areas tree) — the canonical way to configure an area path hierarchy in ADO requires navigating to Project settings > Project configuration > Areas, which is far from the primary workflow surface.

Gaps

  • Rally — keyboard shortcut to open picker: Not documented in the official sources surfaced by this investigation.
  • Rally — URL persistence on project switch: Not documented in the official sources surfaced by this investigation; historical SaaS URLs reportedly include a project OID query param but no canonical doc page confirms this.
  • ADO — search-as-you-type in chrome picker: Not documented in the official sources surfaced by this investigation.
  • ADO — recent / pinned projects in chrome project switcher: Not documented in the official sources surfaced by this investigation.
  • Rally — empty state / no scope selected: Not documented in the official sources surfaced by this investigation.
  • Jira — empty state / no scope selected: Not documented in the official sources surfaced by this investigation.
  • Linear — empty state / no scope selected: Not documented in the official sources surfaced by this investigation.
